observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accounting Office to:
a.	Conduct a thorough review of the affected accounts and ensure that transactions are properly classified in accordance with the Revised Chart of Accounts prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009; and
b. Prepare and process the necessary Journal Entry Vouchers to reclassify misclassified transactions to their appropriate account titles.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the following proposed adjusting journal entries:
To rectify the inappropriate recording of the Interest Income and facilitate its transfer to General Fund; and
To properly recognize the Interest Income during FY 2024 and for prior years.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to identify and verify the reconciling items, gather all supporting documents, and make the necessary adjusting or correcting entries to accurately reflect the adjusted Cash in Bank balance as at December 31, 2024.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to verify and validate the existence of receivables, determine the debtors, and assess their collectability. If deemed uncollectible, request authority to write-off the dormant receivable accounts following COA Circular No. 2016-005. Further, if the receivables lack supporting documents, require the execution of a sworn certification stating the reasons for the loss of documents, and the person/s responsible thereof; and
Ensure that subsidiary ledgers are maintained for receivables in accordance with Section 4(e) of the NGAS to provide detailed information on accounts and facilitate collection efforts in the future.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management:
Direct the Inventory Committee to undertake the physical count of inventory items as at June 30 and December 31 of each year; to prepare the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) by inventory account name and distribute the same as prescribed;  to reconcile said report with the records of the Municipal Accounting Office in accordance with Section 124, Volume I of MNGAS; and to submit to the Office of the Auditor the duplicate copy of the RPCI not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively; and
Direct the CGSO and the Municipal Accountant to maintain updated Stock Cards and Supplies Ledger Cards and regularly reconcile records to detect discrepancies and effect immediate corrections, in compliance with Sections 119 and 120, Volume I, of the MNGAS for LGUs.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accounting Office to reclassify the amount of ₱1,503,195.00 from Guaranty/Security Deposits Payable account to the Customers’ Deposits Payable account;
Direct the Municipal Accounting Office to conduct a review of the outstanding payable, for which the purpose of the bond was already fulfilled, to validate the existence of claims and facilitate their proper disposition or settlement; and
Ensure that the transactions comprising the Guaranty/Security Deposits Payable are described with sufficient particularity to facilitate their settlement or disposition in the future.

We recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office and Municipal Treasury Office take immediate action to settle the outstanding premium contributions from prior years by either remitting payment or coordinating with PhilHealth to verify records, determine the cause/s of the discrepancy, and request any necessary adjustments or corrections. Additionally, if adjustments are required, ensure that all correcting entries are accurately recorded in the books to reflect the reconciled balances.

We recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office conduct an analysis of the Due to GSIS account and coordinate with the GSIS to verify records and identify discrepancies. If necessary, ensure that all correcting entries are accurately recorded in the books to reflect the reconciled balances.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accounting Office and Municipal Treasury Office to take immediate action to settle the unremitted balances by either remitting payment or reconciling the discrepancy between actual withholdings and remittances, and to coordinate with the Pag-IBIG fund to verify employee balances. Additionally, if adjustments are necessary, ensure that all correcting entries are accurately recorded in the books to reflect the correct balances.
We commended the Management for its diligence in ensuring the proper and timely remittance of monthly taxes, demonstrating full compliance with the provisions of Revenue Regulations No. 1-2013.

We recommended that Management:
Advise the Municipal Accounting Office to reclassify the amount of ₱6,286,960.75 from the Due to LGU account to the Trust Liabilities account; and
Review and analyze the unsupported balances totaling ₱416,824.24 and the long-outstanding items totaling ₱87,116.02 for proper disposition and ensure that all transactions are supported by complete and verifiable documentation.

We recommended that Management:
Advise the Municipal Accounting Office to reclassify to the Road Networks account the payment for the Road Right of Way; and
Ensure the timely submission of contracts to COA.

We recommended that Management:
Strictly adhere to the five-day submission requirement under COA Circular No. 2009-001 and ensure that all contracts and supporting documents are transmitted promptly to COA; and
Ensure that procurement documents must be properly supported with approved APPs and PPMPs to ensure compliance with planning requirements under the IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accounting Office to:
Conduct a thorough review of the affected accounts and ensure that transactions are properly recorded in the period in which they occurred, in accordance with Paragraph 7 of IPSAS 1;
Prepare and process the necessary Journal Entry Vouchers to record the accrual of interest expense for December 2024; and
Review prior-year cut-offs to avoid late recognition of expenses in future periods, particularly for loan amortizations.

We recommended that the members of the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) fully implement monitoring tools such as the HGDG and PIMME during GAD planning and budgeting, as well as in the preparation of the accomplishment report. This will ensure the actual expenditures are properly attributed to the GAD budget and that funded programs and projects are genuinely gender-responsive.

We recommended that Management:
Develop and enforce an internal memorandum or directive requiring the concerned office or personnel to submit purchase orders and perfected contracts on time; and
Implement a tracking system for the submission of purchase orders and perfected contracts.

We recommended that Management:
Discontinue the inclusion of salary and benefit appropriations in the Annual Budget for the Sectoral Representatives from the Women and Agricultural Sectors; and
Cease further appointments to these positions until the necessary enabling law and implementing rules and regulations are in place, as required under RA No. 7160 and related issuances.

We recommended that Management secure and attach the prescribed Certification from the Local Chief Executive or Personnel Officer, confirming that the services rendered by JO and COS personnel cannot be provided by regular or permanent staff. This Certification should be included as part of the supporting documents for salary and wage payments, in strict compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and relevant provisions of the COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2, s. 2020.

We commended the Management for its effective implementation of the FY 2024 20% Development Fund, particularly the 100 percent completion rate under Environmental Services and the high implementation rates in Social and Economic Services. Likewise, the substantial obligation of funds under continuing appropriations indicates active progress. We encouraged the continued enhancement of coordination, monitoring, and reporting mechanisms to ensure the timely completion of all remaining PPAs and to sustain the Municipality’s overall performance in fund utilization and development project delivery.

To sustain and further enhance compliance, we recommended that the Local School Board:
Continue adhering to prescribed timelines and initiate the early preparation of required reports to prevent delays;
Validate the accuracy and completeness of SEF reports before submission to ensure reliability; and
Regularly update all members of the Board on reporting and disclosure requirements related to SEF utilization to maintain transparency and accountability.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Require the BAC/BAC Secretariat to immediately submit the lacking documents. Otherwise, we will be constrained to issue a Notice of Suspension pursuant to COA rules and regulations; and
Require the Bids and Awards Committee, to ensure henceforth that all supporting contract documents are complete in compliance with pertinent provisions of R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR, and submitted within five (5) days from execution of contracts in accordance with Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

All designated Collectors/Accountable Officers/deputized Barangay Collectors remit their collections at the end of each business day and refrain from the practice of remitting the collections for a significantly long period of time to avoid misappropriation of cash. In case where travel time from the places of assignment to the Local Treasurer’s Office is more than one day, turnover of collections shall be made once a week or as soon as the collections reach ₱5,000.00; and

The Acting Municipal Treasurer or Liquidating Officer to issue demand letter to Collectors if collections are not remitted at the period required.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the following recommendations:
Direct the Municipality of Ramon Inventory and Disposal Committee to (i) conduct a complete physical count annually as required; (ii) intensify efforts to reconcile the result of physical count with property and accounting records and (iii) recommend for the adjustment of any noted discrepancy in compliance with Paragraph 2 of Section 124, Volume I of MNGAS for LGUs;
Direct the concerned Municipal Officials to maintain and regularly update property cards, subsidiary ledgers, property ledger cards, stock cards and other subsidiary records in conformity with Section 13, Volume II of the MNGAS for LGUs; and
Derecognize unserviceable properties from the books, stop providing depreciation charges for these assets and facilitate disposal of these properties in accordance with Section 79 of PD No. 1445.

We reiterated the following recommendations:
Create an Inventory Committee to undertake the physical count of inventory items as of June 30 and December 31 of each year; to prepare the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) by inventory account name and distribute the same as prescribed;  to reconcile said report with the records of the Municipal Accounting Office in accordance with Section 124, Volume I of MNGAS; and to submit to the Office of the Auditor the duplicate copy of the RPCI not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semesters, respectively.
Direct the Municipal Accountant to correct the errors in recording purchases and issuances of inventories and observe the Perpetual Inventory System as method of recording inventories as prescribed under Section 51, Volume I of the MNGAS for LGUs; and
Instruct the Municipal General Services Officer (GSO) and the Municipal Accountant to maintain updated Stock Cards and Supplies Ledger Cards and regularly reconcile records to detect discrepancies and effect immediate corrections; and for the GSO to promptly prepare and submit the monthly RSMIs to the Municipal Accountant for recording in compliance with Sections 119 and 120, Volume I, of the MNGAS for LGUs.

We recommended that the Municipality of Ramon stop the practice of granting cash advances for purposes beyond the scope prescribed under COA Circular 97-002, strictly observe procurement laws, rules and regulations, and adhere with COA Circular No. 2012-003.

Adapt strict measures to regulate the granting of cash advances in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
Direct the Municipal Accountant to strictly monitor the settlement of unliquidated cash advances and discontinue granting additional cash advances unless all outstanding cash advances have been liquidated. Subsequently, officials and employees with outstanding cash advances who are already out of service should not be cleared from money/property accountability unless liquidations were made.
Stop the granting of cash advances to officers and employees whose position/designation does not conform to the definition of accountable officers as stated in the aforementioned circular.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Bids and Awards Committee, the Municipal Health Office and other concerned officials to adhere with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to ensure the safety and quality of procured drugs and medicines.

We have recommended that Management require the Accounting Department to analyze and ascertain the validity of long outstanding payables.  Those found valid should be paid immediately. On the other hand, recorded payables without valid documentation and which have been outstanding for two or more years and against which no actual claim had been filed should be, with prior concurrence of the Sangguniang Bayan, reverted to Government Equity (unappropriated), to clear payable accounts, as prescribed under Section 98 of PD No. 1445.  Henceforth, only obligations with proper and complete documentation should be recorded in the books of accounts.

We recommended that Management require the concerned officials of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to submit Purchase Orders within five (5) days after its perfection duly supported with all the supporting documents required under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 and a transmittal letter containing a list of supporting documents attached to the advance copy of infrastructure contract/purchase order.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the LDRRM Officer to submit annually a comprehensive Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) compliant to RA 10121 and COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 11, 2002 containing a complete functional classification, list of specific projects and activities with sufficient details of funding whether current year appropriation, continuing appropriation or special trust funds from unexpended DRRMF of prior years, copy furnish the COA Audit Team.  Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor requires the LDDRM Officer to conform with the prescribed format of the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF.
We recommended that the GSO be required to submit the Acceptance and Inspection Reports (AIR) to the Office of the Auditor within twenty four (24) hours from acceptance of procured goods.

We recommended that the Municipality of Ramon stop the practice of charging of expenses against the Special Education Fund for purposes beyond the scope prescribed under Section 272 of RA No. 7160 and Item 4.0 of DepEd-DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 01 series of 2017.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain subsidiary records of all fund transfers from National Government Agencies.   Likewise, direct all concerned personnel who are directly in-charge of the implementation of projects to regularly submit the required reports and complete supporting documents to the Municipal Accountant and the Office of the Auditor in compliance with COA Circular No. 94-013.
We recommended for the immediate return of the unused balances of completed projects to the source agencies to comply with Sections 4.9 and 6.7 of COA Circular 94-013 dated December 13, 1994.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the MRGADC and all the departments shall submit regularly GAD Accomplishment Reports with the corresponding actual costs for particular programs/projects for review and evaluation of accomplishments.

observations.recommendation: We recommended management to: (a) conduct physical inventory at least once a year to determine the whereabouts and conditions of the property of the municipality; (b) update the individual ARE to establish property accountability; (c) prepare individual subsidiary ledger for each type of property; (d) include the reconciliation of accounting records and property records in the duties and functions of the Inventory Team so that the reconciliation of property and accounting records will be performed promptly and effectively.
observations.managementAction:
The Inventory Team spearheaded by the GSO, has already started the conduct of actual physical inventory to the different offices so as to determine the condition of all the properties and equipment of the LGU and to update the existing individual ARE/PAR issued to the accountable offices/employees. Consequently, all unserviceable equipment shall be issued a corresponding  Waste Management Report (WMR), copy furnished the Accounting Office. To possibly reconcile the accounting records with that of the GSO, the Inventory Team which composed of the GSO, Accounting and COA personnel shall reconcile the records based from the actual inventory. Whereas, old property records submitted by the Treasury that have no complete details, such as the date of acquisition and amount shall be given an accurate appraised value. Thus, an Appraisal Team shall be created to perform this task.
In our aim to have systematic Inventory Report, the PAGSO Region 02 initiated the use of a Software-Inventory of PPE/ICS/PAR and Supplies Inventory System. The installing of the system is on-going. We are presently preparing two (20 computers for the system and we would need another one to conform to the specifications needed by the system.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management consider the following: Return the amount of P207,089.02 that was irregularly charged to the 20% development fund and allocate the fund for projects that shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and shall partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures; Utilize the fund to finance priority development projects and programs, as embodied in the duly approved Local Development plan and; Instruct the Municipal Planning Development Officer, Budget Office and the Accountant to adhere strictly with the guidelines on the appropriation and utilization of the 20% economic development fund prescribed under Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 of the DILG and DBM.
observations.managementAction:
Meals, Office Supplies and T-shirts charges were part of the training component of BUB Program, re; Maternal & Child Health Program for the service provider, while other meals and snacks was used for the members of the Municipal Development Council in the formulation of Annual Investment Plan.
There was no Disallowance received from the office of the Sangguniang Panlalawigan regarding the program on that budget year.
The travelling expenses charged to the fund was used by the Municipal Agriculture Office in their inspection program which can also bring Economic impact in the municipality.
Honorarium for the Municipal Zoning Administrator was stopped after the LGU received the 2015 AOM.
Electric consumption was erroneously recorded to the 20% EDF when in fact it was charged to 20% National Wealth.
Various office supplies such as purchase of computer, UPS, among others, in our personal opinion, can be charged to 20% EDF since JMC 2011-01 states that capital expenditures related to the implementation of livelihood or entrepreneurship/ local economic development projects was allowed under the same JMC.
The charging of cleaning supplies and various supplies was misrepresented by such instead of supplies for street sweepers and garbage collectors which could bring impact in the environmental programs of the municipality

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the LGU remit immediately the unexpended balance of the PDAF amounting P116,655.55 to the national government through the Bureau of the Treasury, in conformity with the Supreme Court’s decision.
observations.managementAction: The management already remitted / returned the unexpended balance to the Bureau of Treasury as per DV No. 101-03-1184, dated March 29, 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management require the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to appropriate fund and to charge payment of general and professional services under the General Fund. We also recommended that the Local School Board ensure the equitable distribution of the SEF among the priority areas defined in DECS-DBM-DILG and Section 272 of RA 7160 in the preparation of the annual budget, in order that the Fund’s objectives are achieved.
observations.managementAction:
We adhere to the Commission’s recommendation. Last June 30, 2016, all Job Order (JO) personnel whose payments of services were previously charged to SEF were all terminated. The services of JO personnel hired by the new administration were all charged to General Fund in compliance with the auditor’s recommendation stated in the AOM.
Rest assured that we will strictly comply with provisions provided under Joint Circular No. 01,s.1998 issued by DECS-DBM-DILG and Section 272 of RA 7160.
We furnished all members of Local School Board a copy of this AOM for their reference in the preparation of SEF Budget in the ensuing years.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the municipal officials concerned to strictly comply with Sections 3(j) of RA 10121 and COA Circular 2012-002 to ensure completeness and propriety of claims charged to LDRRMF.
observations.managementAction:
The purchase of 40” flat LED TV charged under LDRRMF was mainly for typhoon monitoring.
Our municipality activates the ICS (Incident Command System) in times of calamity to closely monitor the current situation thus; this justifies the purchase of the said television.
We also adhere to the commission’s recommendation with regards to Janitorial Services. The salaries of rescuers hired by the administration were already charged under General Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management: (a) encourage active participation of barangay officials to support the solid waste management program of the government; (b) complete the City Waste Analysis Characterization Survey (WACS) action plan in order to update the 10-year Solid Management Plan; and (c) assist the barangays technically or in any other manner deemed necessary in order to install and sustain the operation of the MRFs.
observations.managementAction:
The National Solid Waste Management Council has approved the LGU 10 year Solid Waste Management Plan in its Resolution No. 871 series of 2017.
The LGU and barangays conducted and initiated clean-up drive along municipal roads. Also, the LGU acted religiously on the report of the “Basura Patroller” of the EWSM.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management strictly adhere with COA Circular Nos. 74-34, 87-278 and 2009-001.   We also recommended the submission of perfected contracts to the Office of the Audit Team Leader within the prescribed period for proper review and evaluation.
observations.managementAction: The submission of contracts to the COA is partially implemented last year since only Purchase Orders (POs) have been furnished to them. As recommended, the BAC Secretariat are now submitting perfected contracts of the procurement of goods and infrastructure that were subjected for public bidding, small value procurement and Pakyaw for labor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012. We also recommended that management stop incurring the same expenditure on 2017 onward in order to avoid disallowance in audit.
observations.managementAction: The management will adhere to the commission’s recommendation to stop the incurrence of the same expenditure.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management require all out-going officials to turn over the properties issued to them and not to issue clearances from money and property accountabilities that will support their claims for terminal leave pay.

Former elective officials and employees who have not returned the properties and equipment issued to them were not given clearances from money and property accountabilities such as in the case of Former Mayor Wilfredo L.
